system_prompt:
You are Hermes Agent, an intelligent AI assistant created by Nous Research. You are helpful, knowledgeable, and direct. You assist users with a wide range of tasks including answering questions, writing and editing code, analyzing information, creative work, and executing actions via your tools. You communicate clearly, admit uncertainty when appropriate, and prioritize being genuinely useful over being verbose unless otherwise directed below. Be targeted and efficient in your exploration and investigations.

Conversation started: Sunday, April 12, 2026 12:14 AM
Model: anthropic/claude-opus-4.6
Provider: openrouter

You are on a text messaging communication platform, Telegram. Please do not use markdown as it does not render. You can send media files natively: to deliver a file to the user, include MEDIA:/absolute/path/to/file in your response. Images (.png, .jpg, .webp) appear as photos, audio (.ogg) sends as voice bubbles, and videos (.mp4) play inline. You can also include image URLs in markdown format ![alt](url) and they will be sent as native photos.

function.name: patch
function.description:
Targeted find-and-replace edits in files. Use this instead of sed/awk in terminal. Uses fuzzy matching (9 strategies) so minor whitespace/indentation differences won't break it. Returns a unified diff. Auto-runs syntax checks after editing.

Replace mode (default): find a unique string and replace it.
Patch mode: apply V4A multi-file patches for bulk changes.

mode.description: Edit mode: 'replace' for targeted find-and-replace, 'patch' for V4A multi-file patches

path.description: File path to edit (required for 'replace' mode)

old_string.description: Text to find in the file (required for 'replace' mode). Must be unique in the file unless replace_all=true. Include enough surrounding context to ensure uniqueness.

new_string.description: Replacement text (required for 'replace' mode). Can be empty string to delete the matched text.

replace_all.description: Replace all occurrences instead of requiring a unique match (default: false)

patch.description:
V4A format patch content (required for 'patch' mode). Format:
*** Begin Patch
*** Update File: path/to/file
@@ context hint @@
 context line
-removed line
+added line
*** End Patch

function.name: process
function.description: Manage background processes started with terminal(background=true). Actions: 'list' (show all), 'poll' (check status + new output), 'log' (full output with pagination), 'wait' (block until done or timeout), 'kill' (terminate), 'write' (send raw stdin data without newline), 'submit' (send data + Enter, for answering prompts).

action.description: Action to perform on background processes

session_id.description: Process session ID (from terminal background output). Required for all actions except 'list'.

data.description: Text to send to process stdin (for 'write' and 'submit' actions)

timeout.description: Max seconds to block for 'wait' action. Returns partial output on timeout.

offset.description: Line offset for 'log' action (default: last 200 lines)

limit.description: Max lines to return for 'log' action

function.name: read_file
function.description: Read a text file with line numbers and pagination. Use this instead of cat/head/tail in terminal. Output format: 'LINE_NUM|CONTENT'. Suggests similar filenames if not found. Use offset and limit for large files. Reads exceeding ~100K characters are rejected; use offset and limit to read specific sections of large files. NOTE: Cannot read images or binary files — use vision_analyze for images.

path.description: Path to the file to read (absolute, relative, or ~/path)

offset.description: Line number to start reading from (1-indexed, default: 1)

limit.description: Maximum number of lines to read (default: 500, max: 2000)

function.name: search_files
function.description:
Search file contents or find files by name. Use this instead of grep/rg/find/ls in terminal. Ripgrep-backed, faster than shell equivalents.

Content search (target='content'): Regex search inside files. Output modes: full matches with line numbers, file paths only, or match counts.

File search (target='files'): Find files by glob pattern (e.g., '*.py', '*config*'). Also use this instead of ls — results sorted by modification time.

pattern.description: Regex pattern for content search, or glob pattern (e.g., '*.py') for file search

target.description: 'content' searches inside file contents, 'files' searches for files by name

path.description: Directory or file to search in (default: current working directory)

file_glob.description: Filter files by pattern in grep mode (e.g., '*.py' to only search Python files)

limit.description: Maximum number of results to return (default: 50)

offset.description: Skip first N results for pagination (default: 0)

output_mode.description: Output format for grep mode: 'content' shows matching lines with line numbers, 'files_only' lists file paths, 'count' shows match counts per file

context.description: Number of context lines before and after each match (grep mode only)

function.name: terminal
function.description:
Execute shell commands on a Linux environment. Filesystem usually persists between calls.

Do NOT use cat/head/tail to read files — use read_file instead.
Do NOT use grep/rg/find to search — use search_files instead.
Do NOT use ls to list directories — use search_files(target='files') instead.
Do NOT use sed/awk to edit files — use patch instead.
Do NOT use echo/cat heredoc to create files — use write_file instead.
Reserve terminal for: builds, installs, git, processes, scripts, network, package managers, and anything that needs a shell.

Foreground (default): Commands return INSTANTLY when done, even if the timeout is high. Set timeout=300 for long builds/scripts — you'll still get the result in seconds if it's fast. Prefer foreground for short commands.
Background: Set background=true to get a session_id. Two patterns:
  (1) Long-lived processes that never exit (servers, watchers).
  (2) Long-running tasks with notify_on_complete=true — you can keep working on other things and the system auto-notifies you when the task finishes. Great for test suites, builds, deployments, or anything that takes more than a minute.
Use process(action="poll") for progress checks, process(action="wait") to block until done.
Working directory: Use 'workdir' for per-command cwd.
PTY mode: Set pty=true for interactive CLI tools (Codex, Claude Code, Python REPL).

Do NOT use vim/nano/interactive tools without pty=true — they hang without a pseudo-terminal. Pipe git output to cat if it might page.
Important: cloud sandboxes may be cleaned up, idled out, or recreated between turns. Persistent filesystem means files can resume later; it does NOT guarantee a continuously running machine or surviving background processes. Use terminal sandboxes for task work, not durable hosting.

command.description: The command to execute on the VM

background.description: Run the command in the background. Two patterns: (1) Long-lived processes that never exit (servers, watchers). (2) Long-running tasks paired with notify_on_complete=true — you can keep working and get notified when the task finishes. For short commands, prefer foreground with a generous timeout instead.

timeout.description: Max seconds to wait (default: 180). Returns INSTANTLY when command finishes — set high for long tasks, you won't wait unnecessarily.

workdir.description: Working directory for this command (absolute path). Defaults to the session working directory.

check_interval.description: Seconds between automatic status checks for background processes (gateway/messaging only, minimum 30). When set, I'll proactively report progress.

pty.description: Run in pseudo-terminal (PTY) mode for interactive CLI tools like Codex, Claude Code, or Python REPL. Only works with local and SSH backends. Default: false.

notify_on_complete.description: When true (and background=true), you'll be automatically notified when the process finishes — no polling needed. Use this for tasks that take a while (tests, builds, deployments) so you can keep working on other things in the meantime.

function.name: write_file
function.description: Write content to a file, completely replacing existing content. Use this instead of echo/cat heredoc in terminal. Creates parent directories automatically. OVERWRITES the entire file — use 'patch' for targeted edits.

path.description: Path to the file to write (will be created if it doesn't exist, overwritten if it does)

content.description: Complete content to write to the file

messages.content:
You are the Law Firm Wiki compiler. Your job is to read activity logs from 3 PI (personal injury) cases and compile institutional knowledge into structured wiki articles.

STEPS:
1. Read /opt/data/FirmVault/wiki/AGENTS.md (the compiler schema)
2. Read the case files and activity logs for these 3 cases:
   - taylor-thompson (42 logs, negotiation phase)
   - nicole-tewes (76 logs, treatment phase) 
   - samuel-clites (225 logs, negotiation phase)
3. For each case, read the main case file at cases/<slug>/<slug>.md and ALL activity logs in cases/<slug>/Activity Log/ (read them chronologically)
4. Extract patterns, processes, pitfalls, edge cases, and best practices
5. Write concept articles to /opt/data/FirmVault/wiki/concepts/ following the YAML frontmatter schema in AGENTS.md
6. Write connection articles to /opt/data/FirmVault/wiki/connections/
7. Write the index at /opt/data/FirmVault/wiki/index.md
8. Write a compilation log entry at /opt/data/FirmVault/wiki/log.md

IMPORTANT RULES:
- ANONYMIZE all client info. Use 'Case A', 'Case B', 'Case C' instead of real names
- Focus on PATTERNS and PROCESS, not individual case details
- Each concept article must have YAML frontmatter (title, slug, phase, category, tags, confidence, evidence_count, created, updated, related)
- Use [[wikilinks]] between articles
- Confidence: high (5+ cases), medium (2-4 cases), low (1 case)
- Since we only have 3 cases so far, most confidence will be 'low' - that's fine, it will improve as we add more cases
- Write at least 8-12 concept articles covering different phases and categories
- Write at least 2-3 connection articles
- Create a complete index.md organized by phase and category

The activity logs contain real law firm workflow: emails to/from adjusters, call notes, records requests, negotiation correspondence, demand drafting discussions, lien negotiations, PIP claims, and case management tasks. Extract the institutional knowledge embedded in these workflows."
<parameter name="context">The FirmVault is at /opt/data/FirmVault. The wiki schema is at wiki/AGENTS.md. Case files are at cases/<slug>/<slug>.md and activity logs are at cases/<slug>/Activity Log/*.md. For large cases, you may need to read logs in batches. Focus on the imported/filevine logs as they contain the richest content (negotiation emails, demand discussions, treatment updates).

[Subdirectory context discovered: /opt/data/FirmVault/wiki/AGENTS.md]
# Law Firm Wiki — Compiler Schema

> This file is the specification for the LLM that compiles activity logs
> into structured knowledge articles. Read it in full before any compile
> or query operation.

## What You Are

You are a knowledge compiler for a personal injury law firm. Your job is
to read raw case activity logs and extract reusable institutional knowledge
— the process, pitfalls, patterns, and best practices that make a PI
practice work.

You are NOT creating a case database. You are building a **wiki** — a
structured collection of concept articles about HOW PI cases work, derived
from evidence in real cases.

## The Three Operations

### 1. COMPILE (ingest raw → update wiki)

Read activity logs and case files. For each meaningful pattern you identify:

1. Check `index.md` — does a concept article already exist?
2. If yes → UPDATE it: add new evidence, adjust confidence, update Related
3. If no → CREATE it: full article per the Article Schema below
4. If two concepts interact → create/update a Connection article
5. Update `index.md` with any new or modified articles
6. Append a summary to `log.md`

**What to extract:**
- Process patterns (what happens and in what order)
- Decision points (why certain choices were made)
- Problems and resolutions (what went wrong, how it was fixed)
- Timeline patterns (how long things actually take)
- Communication patterns (what works with adjusters, clients, providers)
- Edge cases (unusual situations and how they were handled)
- Anti-patterns (things that caused problems)

**What to IGNORE:**
- Individual client PII (names, SSN, DOB, addresses, phone numbers)
- Specific dollar amounts tied to identifiable cases
- Attorney-client privileged communications
- Raw email addresses and phone numbers
- Replace specifics with anonymized references: "Case A", "Case B", or
  descriptive patterns: "in cases involving [treatment gap > 30 days]"

**Confidence scoring:**
- high: Pattern observed in 5+ cases with consistent outcomes
- medium: Pattern observed in 2-4 cases
- low: Single case observation, or conflicting evidence

### 2. QUERY (ask questions → synthesize answers)

1. Read `index.md` to identify 3-10 relevant concept articles
2. Read those articles in full
3. Synthesize an answer with [[wikilink]] citations
4. If the answer reveals a gap → note it in the answer
5. Optionally file the Q&A back into `wiki/qa/`

### 3. LINT (health check)

Run these checks and write results to `wiki/reports/`:
- **Broken wikilinks**: Links that point to non-existent articles
- **Orphan articles**: Concepts with zero inbound links
- **Phase coverage**: Which PHASE_DAG phases have sparse knowledge
- **Stale articles**: Source data changed since last compile
- **Contradictions**: Articles that contain conflicting guidance

## Article Schema

### Concept Article (wiki/concepts/*.md)

```yaml
---
title: Human-Readable Title
slug: kebab-case-slug
phase: [phase_0_onboarding]    # which PHASE_DAG phases this applies to
category: one-of-below
tags: [tag1, tag2, tag3]
confidence: high               # high/medium/low
evidence_count: 5              # number of cases with supporting evidence
created: YYYY-MM-DD
updated: YYYY-MM-DD
related:                       # other concept slugs
  - related-concept-one
  - related-concept-two
---

# Title

> One-paragraph summary (2-3 sentences max)

## Pattern

Detailed explanation of the pattern, process, or concept.

## Evidence

Anonymized observations from source cases:
- "In N cases where [condition], [outcome]"
- "When [action was taken], [result]"

## Agent Guidance

Actionable instructions for paralegal agents:
1. Step one
2. Step two
3. Step three

## Pitfalls

- Common mistake one
- Common mistake two

## Related

- [[related-concept-one]] — how it connects
- [[related-concept-two]] — how it connects
```

### Categories

- `process`: Standard workflows and procedures
- `adjuster-tactics`: Insurance company behavior patterns
- `edge-cases`: Unusual situations requiring special handling
- `phase-knowledge`: Phase-specific best practices
- `communication`: Client/adjuster/provider communication patterns
- `financial`: Billing, liens, settlements, funding
- `medical`: Treatment patterns, records, chronologies
- `legal`: Litigation, discovery, depositions, trial
- `tools`: Software, systems, and process tools

### Connection Article (wiki/connections/*.md)

Same schema but `category: connection` and the body explains how
two or more concepts interact. Example:

```
treatment-gaps-affect-demand-value.md
→ Links: treatment-gap-defense + demand-timing-strategy
→ Insight: Cases with documented gap explanations recovered 80% of
  expected value vs 50% for unexplained gaps
```

### Q&A Article (wiki/qa/*.md)

```yaml
---
title: Question text
slug: question-slug
date: YYYY-MM-DD
sources:                       # concept articles consulted
  - concept-slug-one
  - concept-slug-two
---

# Question

[Full question text]

## Answer

[Synthesized answer with [[wikilink]] citations]

## Gaps Identified

[Any knowledge gaps the question revealed]
```

## Index Schema (wiki/index.md)

The master catalog. This is the LLM's entry point for all queries.

```markdown
# Law Firm Wiki — Index

Last compiled: YYYY-MM-DD | Articles: N | Connections: N

## By Phase

### Phase 0: Onboarding
| Article | Category | Confidence | Evidence |
|---------|----------|------------|----------|
| [[concepts/onboarding-pitfalls]] | phase-knowledge | high | 15 |

### Phase 1: File Setup
...

## By Category

### Adjuster Tactics
| Article | Phase | Confidence |
|---------|-------|------------|
| [[concepts/treatment-gap-defense]] | 2,3 | high |
...

## Recent Updates

| Date | Article | Action |
|------|---------|--------|
| 2026-04-12 | treatment-gap-defense | created |
```

## Activity Log Format Reference

Activity logs live at `cases/<slug>/Activity Log/*.md` and have this structure:

```yaml
---
schema_version: 2
case_id: uuid
date: "YYYY-MM-DD"
time: "HH:MM:SS"         # optional
category: legal|correspondence|imported|financial|medical|task
subcategory: email|filevine_negotiation|outbound_call|...
---

# Category — Date

**Case:** [[cases/slug/slug|Client Name]]
**Source:** description

[Free-form content: emails, notes, call summaries, task descriptions]
```

### Subcategory Distribution (21K+ logs)
- email: 3,078
- filevine_negotiation: 1,620
- outbound_call: 890
- missed_call: 780
- filevine_treatment: 717
- filevine_demandinprogress: 645
- demand: 534
- filevine_filesetup: 369
- phase_change: 254
- filevine_uim: 179
- inbound_call: 137
- esignature: 78
- call_summary: 10

## PHASE_DAG Reference

The case lifecycle has 8 phases:
- Phase 0: Onboarding (contract, HIPAA, intake)
- Phase 1: File Setup (insurance claims, provider setup)
- Phase 2: Treatment (monitoring, records collection)
- Phase 3: Demand (drafting, review, sending)
- Phase 4: Negotiation (offers, counters, strategy)
- Phase 5: Settlement (agreement, disbursement)
- Phase 6: Lien Resolution (PIP waterfall, lien negotiation)
- Phase 7: Litigation (complaint, discovery, trial)
- Phase 8: Closed

## Rules

1. NEVER include identifiable client information in wiki articles
2. Anonymize all case references (use "Case A", "Case B", or descriptive patterns)
3. Every article must have at least one [[wikilink]] to a related concept
4. Update index.md whenever you create or modify an article
5. Append to log.md whenever you compile
6. Confidence scores must reflect actual evidence count
7. When in doubt about a pattern's validity, use confidence: low
8. Prefer specific actionable guidance over vague advice
9. Group knowledge by PHASE_DAG phase when possible
10. Flag contradictions explicitly — don't silently resolve them

# FirmVault — Agent Orientation

> **Read this in full at the start of every session.** It names the thesis, the non-negotiable rules, and where to find the authoritative docs. Short deliberately — use the imports below for detail.

## What this repo is

FirmVault is the **development environment for an AI paralegal backend** intended to eventually be sold to other personal-injury law firms. This specific repo is Whaley Law Firm's own instance; the architecture we prove here becomes the product.

Two parallel product tracks exist:

1. **This repo** — the GitHub-native paralegal backend: markdown vault, `PHASE_DAG.yaml`, `SKILL.md` library, runtime (materializer + worker), intake pipeline.
2. **Separate Huly fork** (not in this repo) — the lawyer-facing UI. This repo's "UI" is for operators, not lawyers.

## The thesis

**"The firm as a code repo."** Law firm work is repeatable tasks + templates + rules, which is exactly what code is. Put the firm's work into primitives agents already excel at (issues, branches, PRs, diffs) and you get a much better AI experience than any traditional PMS bolt-on.

## Core architectural principles

1. **The vault is a shadow, not the source of truth.** Real files (signed PDFs, audio, emails, faxes) live on the firm's own storage (their server, Dropbox, Box, Drive). The markdown in `cases/<slug>/` is a PHI-masked projection of those files.
2. **Intake is a pipeline, not an agent**: document arrives → real file placed on user's storage → converted to markdown → PHI stripped → opens a PR against this repo. The PR is the first human-in-the-loop gate.
3. **Git is the deliberate interface.** Every agent action is a commit or PR. Free audit trail, free approval gates, trivial rollback. Agents *never* touch real files — only their masked shadows.
4. **Markdown, not a graph DB.** We tried FalkorDB and abandoned it. LLM agents are empirically more reliable on plain text + grep than on Cypher queries. This is a deliberate choice, not laziness.
5. **PHI never lives in the vault.** Placeholders like `{{client_ssn}}` and `{{dob}}` reference secrets held in the user's own infrastructure. At render time, deterministic code (not the model) substitutes real values. The model never sees raw PII.
6. **The vault is the only state store for this layer.** No external database. No per-case JSON files. No FalkorDB. No `${ROSCOE_ROOT}`. See `@skills.tools.workflows/DATA_CONTRACT.md` for the authoritative mapping.

## Do NOT do these things

These are actively deprecated. If you see them in old code or docs, treat them as bugs.

- **Do not reference FalkorDB, Cypher, `graph_client`, `graph_manager`, or `run_cypher_query`.** All removed. Everything reads the vault.
- **Do not reference per-case JSON files** (`overview.json`, `contacts.json`, `insurance.json`, `liens.json`, `expenses.json`, `medical_providers.json`, `case_state.json`). Deleted.
- **Do not reference `${ROSCOE_ROOT}`** or any absolute-path scaffolding. Everything is repo-relative.
- **Do not edit content between `<!-- roscoe-medical-start -->` / `<!-- roscoe-medical-end -->` or `<!-- roscoe-insurance-start -->` / `<!-- roscoe-insurance-end -->` markers.** Those blocks are owned by the import scripts and will be overwritten on next sync.
- **Do not invent new vault paths.** If the thing you want to write doesn't have a home in `DATA_CONTRACT.md`, update the contract first (same commit), then write. Drift between contract and reality is the bug.
- **Do not create top-level dashboards, reports, or state files outside the vault layout**. Operator views read the vault via GitHub API or git; they don't create parallel state.

## Where the authoritative docs live

Import these at launch — they are the contract, not me paraphrasing:

@DESIGN.md

@skills.tools.workflows/DATA_CONTRACT.md

@MEMORY.md

Also non-imported but worth knowing:

- `skills.tools.workflows/workflows/PHASE_DAG.yaml` — machine-readable phase definitions, landmarks, and predicate vocabulary. The materializer reads this.
- `skills.tools.workflows/runtime/README.md` — how the materializer and worker agents drive the task queue.
- `skills.tools.workflows/runtime/task_schema.md` — the contract for task issues (labels, frontmatter, lifecycle).
- `Templates/INDEX.md` — the firm template library (73 slugified templates grouped by category).
- `skills.tools.workflows/Skills/*/SKILL.md` — 42 modernized skills in Anthropic Agent Skills format. Exemplars: `lien-management`, `deposition-prep`, `deposition-strategy`, `pip-application`.

## Slug rules (critical for wikilinks)

From `DATA_CONTRACT.md` §4:

- Lowercase
- Apostrophes and curly quotes **stripped** (no replacement char): `K'daya` → `kdaya`
- `&` → `and`
- All other non-alphanumeric runs collapsed to a single hyphen
- Leading/trailing hyphens stripped

Mismatched slugs break wikilinks across the vault. Slug before you write.

## Runtime model (what's being built)

The system is being turned from passive documentation into an active work-driving loop:

```
Vault (cases/*) ← materializer (cron) → GitHub Issues ← worker (event) → Vault
```

- **Materializer** walks open cases, reads each case's `landmarks:` frontmatter + `PHASE_DAG.yaml`, opens a GitHub Issue for every unsatisfied landmark whose task template preconditions are met. Already prototyped as a prompt in `runtime/materializer_prompt.md`.
- **Worker** is triggered by the `status:ready` label on an issue, claims it, loads the named skill, runs it, commits vault changes that reference the task_id, and flips the issue status. Prototyped in `runtime/worker_prompt.md`.
- **Source of truth** for whether a landmark is satisfied is `case.frontmatter.landmarks.<id>` — the materializer checks this first and only falls back to evaluating a predicate if the key is missing.

## Working branch

All in-progress work lives on `claude/import-cases-from-drive-xiQgL`. Do not push directly to `main` without an explicit request.

## When in doubt

- Read `DATA_CONTRACT.md` before writing to the vault.
- Read `PHASE_DAG.yaml` before touching workflow/landmark logic.
- Read `MEMORY.md` to find out what's in flight and what's pending.
- Check the last 3–5 commits on the working branch to see the most recent decisions.

MEMORY.md is a regular repo file we @-import here. It is NOT the same as

that's machine-local and managed by Claude Code itself. Our repo-level
MEMORY.md is the operational state tracker, committed to git.

# File Transfer from Prior Counsel

> When a client transfers from another firm, obtaining the prior counsel's file is a critical prerequisite before any case work can proceed. This process frequently encounters delays due to unresponsive prior counsel, requiring persistent multi-channel follow-up over days or weeks.

## Pattern

When a client comes to the firm after previously being represented elsewhere, the prior firm's complete file must be obtained before the case can move forward. The typical workflow is:

1. Identify the prior firm and case manager contact info
2. Send an initial email requesting the file
3. Follow up by phone if no response within 1-2 business days
4. Escalate (daily calls, multiple contacts at the prior firm)
5. Enlist client/family assistance if prior firm remains unresponsive
6. Eventually receive file and perform cursory review

The entire process can take 1-2 weeks even with persistent follow-up.

## Evidence

- In Case A, the firm called and emailed the prior counsel (a large national firm) daily for over a week with no response. Multiple contacts at the prior firm were tried. The client's family member was eventually asked to assist. The file was received approximately 11 days after the first request.
- Prior counsel may have already sent a demand, taken certain negotiation steps, or received offers that the new firm needs to understand before proceeding.

## Agent Guidance

1. On day 1 of a transfer case, identify the prior firm's case manager by name and direct contact info
2. Send a formal written request (email) for the complete client file on day 1
3. If no response within 48 hours, begin daily phone calls
4. Log every contact attempt in the activity log
5. After 5 business days of no response, ask the client or their authorized representative to contact the prior firm directly
6. Once the file is received, do a cursory review and report key findings to the attorney: existing demand, offers, treatment gaps, outstanding liens, pre-settlement funding
7. Update the case file with all prior-counsel information before moving to negotiation

## Pitfalls

- Assuming the prior firm will respond quickly — large firms often have slow internal processes
- Not documenting contact attempts — this creates gaps in the activity log
- Proceeding with negotiation before understanding what the prior firm already did (e.g., a demand may have already been sent)
- Missing pre-settlement funding obligations that the prior firm arranged

## Related

- [[demand-sent-by-prior-firm]] — when the prior firm already sent a demand
- [[treatment-gap-defense]] — prior counsel cases often have treatment gaps that adjusters exploit

{"path": "/opt/data/FirmVault/wiki/concepts/treatment-gap-defense.md", "content": "---
title: Treatment Gap as Adjuster Defense

confidence: low

# Treatment Gap as Adjuster Defense

> Insurance adjusters routinely cite gaps in medical treatment as justification for lower settlement offers. A "treatment gap" occurs when a client has a significant period (30+ days) between their initial ER visit or treatment and any follow-up care. This is one of the most common defense tactics in soft-tissue PI cases.

## Pattern

The adjuster's argument follows a predictable logic: if the client was truly injured, they would have sought consistent follow-up treatment. A gap suggests either (a) the injury was not serious, or (b) the client's current complaints are unrelated to the accident. This defense is particularly effective when:

- Client only had a single ER visit with no follow-up
- Client was recommended for further treatment (chiropractic, PT) but did not follow through
- The gap spans several months between the accident and any subsequent care

The treatment gap defense directly reduces the perceived value of the case and is often the primary justification for low initial offers.

## Evidence

- In Case A (negotiation phase), the adjuster explicitly cited "the gap in treatment" as justification for the offer amount. The client had treatment at only one provider with no additional follow-up care.
- In Case B (treatment phase), the client's doctor recommended chiropractic care, but the client decided against it due to difficulty coordinating treatment around work and parenting. The client's spouse acknowledged this "might be an area of risk."

## Agent Guidance

1. During treatment monitoring, flag any client who has not followed up with recommended care within 30 days
2. If a client indicates they are stopping treatment, document the reason clearly in the activity log
3. When preparing the demand, proactively address any treatment gap with an explanation (work constraints, childcare obligations, incarceration, etc.)
4. Advise the attorney when a treatment gap exceeds 30 days so they can counsel the client about the impact on case value
5. In negotiation, prepare counterarguments to the gap defense before the adjuster raises it

## Pitfalls

- Failing to document the client's reason for not pursuing recommended treatment
- Not flagging the gap early enough for the attorney to address it with the client
- Allowing the adjuster's gap argument to go unchallenged in negotiation
- Not distinguishing between treatment gaps caused by client choice vs. external circumstances (incarceration, financial constraints, scheduling difficulties)

## Related

- [[client-declines-recommended-treatment]] — when clients choose not to follow through on provider recommendations
- [[demand-value-factors]] — treatment continuity affects demand valuation
- [[pip-claims-management]] — PIP benefits may still be available to cover follow-up care
"}

---

# PIP Claims Management

> Personal Injury Protection (PIP) is a first-party insurance benefit (from the client's own policy or the at-fault party's policy in KY) that covers medical bills regardless of fault. Managing PIP claims is a critical parallel workflow that runs alongside the BI claim. PIP must be actively directed to pay bills, and its ledger must be tracked for demand preparation.

## Pattern

PIP management follows this lifecycle:

1. **Setup**: Send Letter of Representation to PIP carrier with claim number during file setup
2. **Bill Routing**: As medical bills come in, forward them to PIP with a "direction to pay" (explicit instruction to process)
3. **Ledger Tracking**: Periodically request PIP ledger updates to track what has been paid
4. **Demand Integration**: The PIP ledger is needed before sending the BI demand to understand total medical specials and what remains unpaid
5. **Exhaust Monitoring**: In some cases, PIP benefits may exhaust before all bills are paid

Key observations from case data:
- PIP carriers may sit on submitted bills for extended periods (30+ days)
- Bills sometimes need to be re-sent when PIP does not process them
- PIP adjusters may respond that they "still have benefits reserved" but have only one bill on file
- Health insurance deductibles interact with PIP: bills may first go through health insurance, with the remainder then submitted to PIP
- Indiana MVAs do NOT have PIP coverage (jurisdiction matters)

## Evidence

- In Case C, bills were sent to State Farm PIP on 11/1/2024, but as of 12/31/2024 (60 days later), NO PAYMENTS had been made by PIP. The attorney directed staff to resend and call PIP to identify the issue.
- In Case B, the PIP adjuster from Erie Insurance responded to a ledger request noting "you still have benefits reserved" and only had one bill on file, despite multiple provider bills existing. Additional bills needed to be forwarded with direction to process.
- In Case B, the PIP adjuster explained that one hospital bill was "applied to deductible" by health insurance, which is why PIP issued payment only to the radiology provider.
- In Case A, the case was an Indiana MVA, meaning no PIP was available — this is a jurisdiction-specific distinction.

## Agent Guidance

1. Check jurisdiction first — Indiana MVAs do not have PIP; Kentucky MVAs do
2. During file setup, send LOR to PIP carrier and request acknowledgment
3. As each medical bill is received, forward to PIP with explicit "please process the attached and forward an updated PIP ledger upon completion"
4. Set a 30-day follow-up task for each bill submitted to PIP
5. If no PIP payment is confirmed within 30 days, resend the bill AND call the PIP adjuster
6. Request PIP ledger updates before demand preparation
7. Track which bills have been paid by PIP vs. health insurance vs. unpaid
8. When health insurance pays part of a bill, understand the deductible interaction before submitting to PIP

## Pitfalls

- Assuming PIP will automatically process bills — they require explicit direction to pay
- Sending bills once and not following up for months
- Not tracking which bills PIP has vs. doesn't have on file
- Forgetting that out-of-state accidents may not have PIP coverage
- Not understanding the health insurance deductible / PIP interaction

## Related

- [[medical-bill-routing-workflow]] — the overall process for routing medical bills
- [[pip-ledger-requests]] — specifically requesting and tracking PIP ledgers
- [[treatment-gap-defense]] — unpaid bills and PIP delays can affect case timeline
"}

---

# Medical Records and Bills Collection

> Collecting complete medical records and bills from all treatment providers is a prerequisite for demand preparation. This process involves sending HIPAA authorizations, formal record/bill requests via fax and email, and persistent follow-up when providers are slow to respond. Gaps in records collection directly delay case resolution.

## Pattern

The records collection workflow follows this sequence:

1. **Provider Identification**: Confirm all treatment providers with the client, including ER, PCP, specialists, imaging
2. **Request Preparation**: Generate HIPAA medical authorization forms and formal records/billing request letters for each provider
3. **Request Delivery**: Send via fax (using RingCentral or similar) and/or email to the provider's records department
4. **Response Tracking**: Track which providers have responded with records vs. bills vs. both
5. **Follow-Up**: If no response within 2-3 weeks, follow up
6. **Client Assistance**: Ask clients to help obtain records from their patient portal (MyChart) when formal requests are delayed
7. **Completeness Check**: Verify all providers are accounted for before moving to demand

## Evidence

- In Case B, records/billing requests were sent to St. Elizabeth Florence Hospital via email to a specific contact (Dawn.Probst@stelizabeth.com). The billing info was returned same day. However, getting complete records from Christ Hospital Physicians Primary Care took significantly longer.
- In Case C, records were requested from Baptist Health Louisville via fax on 4/29/2024. Records were received by 5/13/2024 (14 days). A task to "review records and add to Timeline" was created once received.
- In Case B, the client's spouse provided medical visit details from the patient portal (MyChart) while waiting for formal records, which helped move the case forward.
- In Case C, the attorney flagged that records collection had stalled with "no action" for a month, emphasizing that negotiation cannot proceed without complete records.

## Agent Guidance

1. Create a provider checklist during file setup with records requested/received dates for each
2. Send HIPAA authorizations and record requests within the first week of file setup
3. Use both fax and email when possible — some providers respond faster to one channel
4. Set follow-up tasks at 14-day intervals for each outstanding request
5. Encourage clients to share records from patient portals as an interim measure
6. Track records and bills separately — receiving one does not mean you have the other
7. Do not move to demand preparation until ALL providers have been accounted for
8. When the attorney asks about case status, have the records collection status ready

## Pitfalls

- Not confirming all providers with the client early (discovering a provider late delays everything)
- Sending requests and not tracking responses
- Not distinguishing between records received and bills received (you need both)
- Letting the client's medical portal records substitute for formal provider records in the demand
- Waiting too long to follow up on non-responsive providers

## Related

- [[pip-claims-management]] — PIP needs bills to process; records collection feeds PIP workflow
- [[demand-timing-strategy]] — demand cannot be sent until records are complete
- [[provider-communication-patterns]] — how different providers respond to requests
"}

---

# Client Communication Through Family Members

> In many PI cases, the primary point of contact is a family member (spouse, parent) rather than the injured client directly. This pattern creates both efficiencies (an engaged advocate) and risks (communication gaps, authorization requirements). Managing these relationships requires clear protocols around authorization, documentation, and expectation-setting.

## Pattern

Two common scenarios emerge:

**Spouse as Primary Contact**: The spouse handles all communication, forwards medical documents, asks status questions, and relays information to the injured client. The injured client may have signed the retainer but is less directly involved in day-to-day case management.

**Parent with POA**: When a client is incarcerated or otherwise unavailable, a parent with Power of Attorney may serve as the authorized representative. The POA document must be obtained and verified before the firm can discuss case details.

In both scenarios, the family member tends to:
- Be more proactive about asking for updates
- Forward bills and medical documents as they arrive
- Express urgency about resolution ("we want this to go away")
- Ask about the impact of unpaid bills on credit

## Evidence

- In Case B, the client's husband was the primary communicator throughout the entire case. He forwarded all medical bills, relayed appointment results, asked about case status repeatedly, and expressed concern about bills going to collections. The client herself was largely absent from communications.
- In Case A, the client was incarcerated and the client's mother (with POA) was the sole point of contact. The mother provided intake information, the POA document, and followed up on case status.

## Agent Guidance

1. During intake, identify who the primary point of contact will be
2. If it's a family member, verify authorization: POA for non-spouse, general authorization for spouse
3. Document the authorized contact in the case file with their contact info
4. Set expectations with the family member about case timeline and the process
5. When the family member forwards bills/records, acknowledge receipt promptly and explain next steps
6. When the family member expresses urgency, respond with specific status updates rather than vague reassurances
7. If bills are going to collections, explain the process (PIP submission, timeline) proactively

## Pitfalls

- Discussing case details with a family member who has not been properly authorized
- Assuming the family member is accurately relaying information to/from the client
- Not setting expectations with the family member about realistic case timelines
- Ignoring repeated follow-up inquiries from family members (this erodes trust)
- Not having a clear protocol for who can authorize settlement decisions

## Related

- [[client-expectations-management]] — managing expectations is crucial when family members push for faster resolution
- [[intake-via-referral]] — cases often come through family referrals
"}

# Demand Sent by Prior Firm

> When a client transfers from another firm that already sent a demand letter, the new firm inherits a negotiation position they did not create. This complicates strategy because the new firm must understand the demand's terms, the adjuster's response, and any existing offers before proceeding.

## Pattern

This edge case creates several challenges:

1. The new firm may not know the demand amount or its basis without the prior firm's file
2. An offer may already exist (the client may have reported it during intake)
3. The new firm cannot simply send a new demand — the adjuster already has one
4. The case may be in "negotiation" phase from day one, skipping the firm's normal demand preparation workflow
5. The attorney may need to "wrap up" the case rather than run a full demand cycle

## Evidence

- In Case A, the client reported an existing offer (described as approximately $18K) from the prior firm's negotiation. When the new firm's attorney reviewed the file, he noted "Demand was sent by [prior firm]" and "It is in negotiation but I don't see a demand." This created confusion about whether a formal demand existed or just an offer. The attorney then asked the paralegal to "wrap this one up."

## Agent Guidance

1. During intake for transfer cases, ask the client: "Has your prior attorney sent a demand? Have you received any offers?"
2. When the prior firm's file arrives, locate the demand letter and any offer correspondence immediately
3. Report to the attorney: demand amount, offer amount, adjuster's stated reasons for the offer level
4. The attorney decides whether to renegotiate from the existing position or take other action
5. Do not send a new demand without attorney approval — this could reset negotiations unfavorably

## Pitfalls

- Not asking about prior demand/offers during intake
- Attempting to start the demand process from scratch when a demand already exists
- Not understanding the pre-existing negotiation dynamics
- Moving the case to "negotiation" phase without a demand in the new firm's file (confuses internal tracking)

## Related

- [[file-transfer-from-prior-counsel]] — obtaining the prior firm's file is the prerequisite
- [[demand-timing-strategy]] — normal demand timing doesn't apply in transfer cases
- [[settlement-offer-evaluation]] — evaluating the existing offer in context
"}

# Health Insurance Lien Management

> When a PI client has health insurance that pays accident-related medical bills, the health insurer asserts a subrogation lien — a right to be reimbursed from any settlement. Managing these liens involves verifying their accuracy, removing unrelated charges, and ultimately negotiating a reduction. Liens directly reduce the client's net recovery.

## Pattern

The health insurance lien lifecycle:

1. **Identification**: The health insurer sends a "notice of subrogation claim" or "subrogation interest" letter once they learn of the accident
2. **Itemization Request**: Request a detailed itemization of the lien showing each date of service and amount paid
3. **Accuracy Audit**: Compare the lien itemization against the case's actual accident-related treatment
4. **Dispute Unrelated Charges**: If the lien includes charges for treatment unrelated to the accident (pre-existing conditions, unrelated medical visits), request removal with supporting documentation
5. **Updated Ledger**: Once disputed items are removed, obtain an updated lien ledger
6. **Settlement Integration**: Factor the adjusted lien into the settlement math
7. **Negotiation**: After settlement, negotiate a reduction of the lien (typically firms negotiate a percentage reduction)

## Evidence

- In Case C, the health insurer (Anthem) asserted a subrogation lien that included a 5/16/2024 date of service for an angiogram. The attorney identified that this procedure was scheduled prior to the accident (on 4/10/2024) for a pre-existing cardiac condition and was unrelated to the MVA. The paralegal emailed the lien handler (Carelon) requesting removal of the unrelated dates of service. An updated lien ledger with the charges removed was received the same day.

## Agent Guidance

1. When a subrogation notice arrives, log it immediately and request a detailed itemization
2. Compare every date of service on the lien against the client's accident-related treatment dates
3. Flag any dates of service that fall outside the treatment window or relate to unrelated conditions
4. Draft a dispute letter to the lien handler citing the specific dates and reasons for removal
5. Obtain pre-accident medical records if needed to prove a condition was pre-existing
6. Keep the updated lien ledger in the case file for settlement calculations
7. Alert the attorney whenever the lien amount changes materially

## Pitfalls

- Accepting the lien amount at face value without auditing it
- Not knowing the client's pre-existing medical history (makes it impossible to identify unrelated charges)
- Delaying the lien dispute — the longer you wait, the harder it is to resolve before settlement
- Confusing the health insurance lien with PIP payments (they are separate obligations)
- Not getting the updated lien ledger in writing after charges are removed

## Related

- [[pip-claims-management]] — PIP payments reduce what health insurance pays (and thus the lien)
- [[demand-value-factors]] — the lien amount affects net recovery and settlement strategy
- [[pre-existing-condition-separation]] — separating accident-related from pre-existing treatment
"}

# Pre-Settlement Funding Obligations

> Some PI clients obtain "pre-settlement funding" (lawsuit loans) from third-party lenders while their case is pending. These loans create obligations that must be satisfied from the settlement proceeds, reducing the client's net recovery. The firm must understand its obligation to the funder and factor the loan into settlement math.

## Pattern

Pre-settlement funding creates a complex financial picture at settlement:

1. Client takes a loan from a funding company (e.g., "Claims Angel") during the pendency of the case
2. The loan accrues interest and fees over time
3. At settlement, the funding must be repaid from the client's share of proceeds
4. The attorney must factor the loan payoff into the disbursement sheet

Settlement math with pre-settlement funding:
- Gross settlement
- Less: Attorney fees
- Less: Case costs
- Less: Medical provider payments/liens
- Less: Pre-settlement funding payoff
- Equals: Client's net recovery

When the settlement offer is modest, pre-settlement funding can consume a large portion of what's left, leaving the client with very little.

## Evidence

- In Case A, the client had "loans" from a company named "Claims Angel" totaling $6,537.05. With an $18K offer, after paying the loan ($6,537.05) and 50% of the medical provider bill ($5,397.50), and attorney fees, the client would receive less than $3K. The paralegal noted: "I'm not sure what obligation we have to satisfy the presettlement funding."

## Agent Guidance

1. During intake, ask if the client has obtained any pre-settlement funding or lawsuit loans
2. If yes, request the funding agreement and current payoff amount
3. Log the funder name, original loan amount, and current payoff in the case financial records
4. Factor the funding payoff into all settlement evaluations
5. When presenting settlement options to the client, show the complete disbursement breakdown including the funding payoff
6. Ask the attorney about the firm's legal obligation to satisfy the funding (varies by jurisdiction and agreement)
7. If the settlement barely covers obligations, flag this to the attorney early

## Pitfalls

- Not discovering the pre-settlement funding until settlement disbursement
- Not understanding the firm's legal obligation to the funder
- Not factoring funding into the settlement evaluation, leading to unrealistic client expectations
- Assuming the payoff amount is the same as the original loan (interest and fees accrue)

## Related

- [[settlement-offer-evaluation]] — funding obligations affect whether an offer is adequate
- [[demand-value-factors]] — knowing the funding obligation influences demand strategy
- [[lien-management-health-insurance]] — liens and funding together determine net recovery
"}

# Case Phase Transitions

> PI cases follow a defined lifecycle: Lead -> Potential New Client -> File Setup -> Treatment -> Demand in Progress -> Negotiation -> Settlement. Each transition has specific prerequisites and is triggered by specific events. Understanding these transitions is critical for case management automation.

## Pattern

Observed phase transitions across cases:

### Lead -> File Setup (or via Potential New Client)
- **Trigger**: Intake docs signed, client info received
- **Prerequisites**: Signed fee agreement, HIPAA authorization, basic client info
- **Actions on transition**: Create welcome call task, send welcome email, set up insurance claims, begin records requests
- **Timeline**: Same day to a few days after initial contact

### File Setup -> Treatment
- **Trigger**: All providers confirmed, initial records/bills requests sent
- **Prerequisites**: Insurance claims set up, LOR sent, medical providers identified
- **Actions on transition**: Begin treatment monitoring, send records requests
- **Timeline**: Days after file setup

### Treatment -> Demand in Progress
- **Trigger**: Client confirms treatment has concluded, or attorney directs
- **Prerequisites**: Treatment complete, preferably all records received
- **Actions on transition**: Confirm treatment status with client, request outstanding records/bills, begin demand preparation
- **Timeline**: Weeks to months after treatment ends (waiting for records)

### Demand in Progress -> Negotiation
- **Trigger**: Demand package sent to adjuster
- **Prerequisites**: All records/bills received, demand drafted and approved
- **Note**: In transfer cases, may skip directly to Negotiation

## Evidence

- In Case A, the case went directly from Lead to File Setup to Negotiation (skipping Treatment and Demand) because the prior firm had already handled treatment and sent a demand.
- In Case B, the transition from Treatment to Demand in Progress was triggered by the attorney noting "no activity since August" and directing staff to check in with the client. Once treatment was confirmed complete, the phase change occurred.
- In Case C, the case went through Lead -> File Setup -> Treatment sequentially, with clear phase change entries in the activity log.

## Agent Guidance

1. Track phase change prerequisites as a checklist
2. Do not advance a phase until all prerequisites are met
3. Log every phase change with the person who authorized it and the date
4. When an attorney asks about a case, report the current phase and what's blocking the next transition
5. For transfer cases, evaluate which phases were already completed by the prior firm
6. The attorney should approve all phase transitions from Demand in Progress onward

## Pitfalls

- Advancing to Negotiation without a demand in the file (observed in Case A)
- Not confirming treatment completion with the client before transitioning to Demand
- Letting cases sit in Treatment for months without checking on client status
- Not logging the reason for the phase change

## Related

- [[medical-records-collection]] — records completion is a prerequisite for Demand
- [[demand-timing-strategy]] — timing of the phase transition to Demand matters
- [[attorney-review-triggers]] — certain transitions require attorney review
"}

# Attorney Review Triggers

> Certain events in a PI case require the attorney's direct involvement and approval. These are decision points where paralegal-level handling is insufficient and attorney judgment is needed. Identifying and routing these triggers efficiently prevents bottlenecks and ensures quality control.

## Pattern

Attorney review is triggered by:

1. **Initial Case Evaluation**: When a transfer case file arrives, the attorney reviews the prior firm's work, demand status, and financial picture before directing next steps
2. **Stale Case Flag**: When the attorney notices no activity on a case for an extended period (2+ months), they direct staff to check in with the client
3. **Lien Disputes**: When unrelated charges appear on a health insurance lien, the attorney directs which items to dispute
4. **Settlement Authority**: Client gives authority to "negotiate to LBF" (Last Best and Final) — the attorney must approve the negotiation range
5. **Case Complexity Flags**: Pre-settlement funding obligations, minimal treatment, incarcerated clients — these require attorney guidance on strategy
6. **Demand Approval**: Before a demand is sent, the attorney reviews and approves
7. **File Completion Escalation**: When records collection stalls, the attorney escalates to ensure action

## Evidence

- In Case A, the attorney reviewed the transfer case and identified key issues: prior demand already sent, treatment gap, pre-settlement funding, and minimal treatment. He then directed staff on next steps.
- In Case B, the attorney flagged the case noting "no activity since the middle of August" and directed a check-in with the client. Later, the attorney changed the phase from Treatment to Demand in Progress.
- In Case C, the attorney identified that unrelated cardiac treatment was included in the Anthem lien and directed its removal. He also escalated when records requests had "no action" for a month.

## Agent Guidance

1. Route the following to the attorney immediately:
   - New transfer case files received (with summary memo)
   - Settlement offers received
   - Lien itemizations with potential disputes
   - Client requests for case status update (if case has been inactive 30+ days)
   - Bills going to collections
   - Pre-settlement funding documentation
2. When routing to the attorney, include a brief summary and recommended action
3. Track attorney-pending items and follow up if no response within 48 hours
4. Document the attorney's direction in the activity log verbatim

## Pitfalls

- Not routing complex issues to the attorney (trying to handle them at paralegal level)
- Routing too many routine items to the attorney (creating bottlenecks)
- Not including sufficient context when routing (attorney has to dig through the file)
- Not following up on items pending attorney review
- Not documenting the attorney's direction

## Related

- [[case-phase-transitions]] — phase changes from Demand onward require attorney approval
- [[settlement-offer-evaluation]] — offers always require attorney evaluation
- [[lien-management-health-insurance]] — lien disputes require attorney direction
"}

# Client Expectations Management

> PI clients (and their family members) frequently become anxious about case timelines, especially when medical bills go to collections. Proactively managing expectations about the process, timeline, and their role prevents client dissatisfaction and premature settlement pressure.

## Pattern

Client/family anxiety follows a predictable escalation:

1. **Early Phase (0-3 months)**: Client is patient, providing information as requested
2. **Mid Phase (3-6 months)**: Client begins asking "Is there anything we need to be doing?" and "Are we close to resolving this?"
3. **Late Phase (6+ months)**: Client receives collection notices and expresses urgency — "I don't want this to hurt our credit" and "We want this to go away"
4. **Critical Point**: Client may pressure for quick settlement at any amount to end the process

The firm's response pattern is typically reactive: responding to inquiries with brief status updates. Proactive communication is rare in the observed data.

## Evidence

- In Case B, the client's husband followed up approximately monthly with increasing urgency. His communications escalated from "Just checking in" (August) to "Do you feel like we are close?" (November) to "We received a letter from a debt collection agency... We want this to go away" (November) to "When can we reach a resolution? We really want to put this to bed" (December).
- In Case A, the client's mother followed up asking "he was asking if I have heard anything about his case" after months of apparent inactivity.

## Agent Guidance

1. Set expectations at intake: explain the typical case timeline (6-18 months for most PI cases)
2. Send proactive status updates at least monthly, even if the update is "waiting for records"
3. When bills go to collections, explain immediately: "We have submitted this to PIP for processing. It may take up to 30 days. The collection activity will be resolved through the settlement."
4. When a client asks "are we close?", give a specific answer: "We need X, Y, and Z before we can send the demand. I expect that to take approximately N weeks."
5. Never leave a client inquiry unanswered for more than 24 hours
6. Document client expectations and concerns in the activity log

## Pitfalls

- Being reactive instead of proactive with client communication
- Giving vague responses like "Not at this time" without explaining what the timeline looks like
- Ignoring the emotional impact of collection notices on clients
- Allowing months to pass without any client contact
- Not explaining the process steps that remain before resolution

## Related

- [[client-communication-through-family]] — many of these interactions happen through family members
- [[medical-bill-collections-anxiety]] — collections are the primary driver of client urgency
- [[case-phase-transitions]] — understanding the phase helps set realistic expectations
"}

# Medical Bills Going to Collections

> A recurring pain point in PI cases is medical bills going to collection agencies while the case is pending. This causes significant client anxiety about credit impact and creates urgency to resolve the case. The firm must manage both the practical issue (getting bills paid through PIP or other channels) and the emotional impact on the client.

## Pattern

The collections problem follows a timeline:

1. Accident occurs, client receives ER treatment
2. Hospital/provider bills health insurance first
3. If health insurance doesn't cover (or only partially covers), the provider bills the client
4. After 60-90 days of non-payment, the provider sends the bill to a collection agency
5. The collection agency contacts the client
6. Client contacts the firm in distress about credit impact

The gap between accident and collections is typically 3-6 months. This often coincides with the period when the firm is still collecting records and bills, or waiting for PIP to process.

## Evidence

- In Case C, the client brought in collection notices from Credit Bureau Systems for the Baptist Health bill. He was "concerned because he does not want that to impact his credit — as he needs a replacement vehicle." The bill had been submitted to PIP on 11/1/2024 but PIP had made no payments as of 12/31/2024.
- In Case B, the client's husband forwarded a "notice from a debt collection agency" for a radiology bill and a "letter from a debt collection agency" for another bill, asking "Shouldn't they be aware that this is an accident claim?" The firm responded by forwarding bills to PIP for processing.

## Agent Guidance

1. Proactively warn clients at intake that medical bills may go to collections during the case, and this is normal
2. When a bill is submitted to PIP, note the submission date and set a 30-day follow-up
3. If PIP has not processed within 30 days, escalate before the bill goes to collections
4. When a client reports a collection notice, respond within 24 hours with:
   - Acknowledgment of their concern
   - Status of the bill submission to PIP/insurance
   - Expected timeline for resolution
   - Reassurance that this will be addressed through the settlement
5. Request a PIP payment log/ledger to demonstrate to the collection agency that the bill is being processed
6. If possible, contact the collection agency with the firm's LOR and documentation of PIP processing

## Pitfalls

- Dismissing client concerns about collections as unimportant
- Not submitting bills to PIP proactively (waiting for clients to forward bills)
- Slow response to collection notices (increases client anxiety)
- Not following up with PIP when they fail to process bills timely
- Not explaining to clients that accident-related bills follow a different payment path

## Related

- [[pip-claims-management]] — PIP is the primary mechanism for paying bills that otherwise go to collections
- [[client-expectations-management]] — collections anxiety is a major driver of client dissatisfaction
- [[medical-records-collection]] — timely bill collection helps route bills to PIP faster
"}

# Intake and Onboarding Workflow

> The intake process converts a potential client into an active case. It involves collecting personal information, executing the retainer agreement, setting up insurance claims, identifying medical providers, and establishing communication channels. The workflow must be completed rapidly to preserve evidence and begin the claims process.

## Pattern

Standard intake workflow observed across cases:

1. **Initial Contact**: Client (or family member) reaches out, usually by phone. Case manager gathers preliminary accident details, injuries, and insurance information.
2. **Information Request**: Firm requests: Name, DOB, SSN, phone number, copy of health insurance card, names/addresses of hospital and PCP.
3. **Contract Execution**: Intake documents sent via VineSign (electronic signature platform) for completion. Documents include fee agreement, HIPAA authorization, and other intake forms.
4. **Welcome Call**: Once docs are signed, firm conducts a welcome call to confirm treatment providers and introduce the client to the process.
5. **Phase Change**: Case moves from Lead/PNC to File Setup.
6. **Insurance Setup**: Send Letters of Representation to both BI (at-fault) and PIP (client's own) carriers. Request acknowledgment.
7. **Provider Setup**: Add all medical providers to the system. Send records/billing requests.
8. **Phase Change**: Case moves from File Setup to Treatment.

Timeline: This entire workflow typically spans 3-7 days from initial contact.

## Evidence

- In Case B, the initial contact came from the client's husband who provided detailed accident information, insurance claim numbers, and adjuster contacts. The firm requested personal info (DOB, SSN, etc.), sent VineSign intake docs, and received signed docs within 3 days.
- In Case C, the intake and welcome call happened on day 1 (4/25/2024). Insurance claims were set up and LORs sent the same day. Records requests went out by 4/29.
- In Case A, intake was complicated by the prior firm transfer — mom signed intake docs via VineSign, but the file transfer from the prior firm took over a week.

## Agent Guidance

1. Request all necessary information in the first communication (don't spread it across multiple contacts)
2. Send VineSign intake docs within 24 hours of initial contact
3. If docs are not signed within 48 hours, follow up
4. Conduct the welcome call within 24 hours of signed docs
5. Set up insurance claims (LORs to BI and PIP) within 48 hours of signed docs
6. Identify and add all medical providers within the first week
7. Log the phase change from Lead to File Setup once docs are signed
8. For referral cases (husband calling for wife, etc.), get the actual client to sign the docs

## Pitfalls

- Slow VineSign turnaround — follow up if not signed within 48 hours
- Playing phone tag with the client for days during intake (use text/email as backup)
- Not capturing all insurance information (both BI and PIP carriers) in the initial contact
- Not asking about all medical providers during the welcome call
- Not sending LORs promptly (this delays adjuster acknowledgment and can delay everything downstream)

## Related

- [[case-phase-transitions]] — intake triggers the first phase transitions
- [[client-communication-through-family]] — intake often involves a family member
- [[file-transfer-from-prior-counsel]] — transfer cases have a modified intake workflow
"}

{"path": "/opt/data/FirmVault/wiki/concepts/statute-of-limitations-monitoring.md", "content": "---
title: Statute of Limitations Monitoring
slug: statute-of-limitations-monitoring

tags: [sol, statute-of-limitations, deadline, compliance, risk]

---

# Statute of Limitations Monitoring

> The statute of limitations (SOL) is the absolute deadline for filing a lawsuit. In Kentucky PI cases, this is typically 2 years from the date of accident. Approaching the SOL without resolution requires escalation to the attorney for a decision: settle at current offer, file suit, or withdraw. SOL monitoring is automated in Filevine with countdown alerts.

## Pattern

SOL monitoring follows a countdown pattern with escalation triggers:

- **180 days out**: Initial alert — ensure case is actively being worked
- **90 days out**: Elevated alert — settlement or suit filing decision must be imminent
- **30 days out**: Critical alert — attorney must decide: file suit, accept offer, or take other action

Cases that are still in negotiation as the SOL approaches create high-risk situations. The firm must either:
1. Reach settlement before the SOL expires
2. File a lawsuit to preserve the claim (can still settle after filing)
3. Obtain a tolling agreement from the defense

## Evidence

- In Case C, the SOL countdown appeared in the activity log: "30 day(s) to SOL in Case Summary (as of 3/24/2026)" with the actual SOL date of 4/23/2026 (exactly 2 years from the 4/23/2024 accident date). Prior alerts at 180 and 90 days were also logged and struck through as passed.

## Agent Guidance

1. Calculate the SOL date at intake and enter it in the case management system
2. Verify the SOL calculation: in Kentucky, PI is 2 years from date of accident
3. At 180 days out, flag the case to the attorney with current status
4. At 90 days out, escalate: what is the plan for resolution?
5. At 30 days out, the attorney must make a decision: settle, file suit, or obtain tolling agreement
6. Never let a case approach SOL without attorney awareness
7. For multi-state cases, verify the correct jurisdiction's SOL (it may differ)

## Pitfalls

- Miscalculating the SOL date (especially for out-of-state accidents)
- Relying solely on automated alerts without manual verification
- Allowing a case to stall in negotiation as SOL approaches
- Not escalating to the attorney early enough to prepare a complaint if needed
- Assuming the SOL can be extended without a formal agreement

## Related

- [[case-phase-transitions]] — SOL creates hard deadlines for phase progression
- [[attorney-review-triggers]] — SOL approach is a mandatory attorney review trigger
"}

# Treatment Gaps Affect Settlement Value

> This connection documents how [[treatment-gap-defense]] interacts with [[client-expectations-management]] and [[pre-settlement-funding-obligations]] to create cases where the client receives minimal net recovery despite having a valid claim.

## Connection

Two concepts converge to create low-value outcomes:

1. **Treatment Gap Defense** ([[treatment-gap-defense]]): The adjuster uses the gap in treatment to justify a lower offer. The client may have stopped treatment due to practical reasons (incarceration, work/parenting constraints) rather than lack of injury.

2. **Client Declines Treatment** ([[client-declines-recommended-treatment]]): When a doctor recommends follow-up care (chiropractic, PT) but the client does not follow through, it creates the very treatment gap the adjuster will exploit.

The interaction creates a vicious cycle: the client's circumstances prevent treatment, the lack of treatment lowers the case value, and the lower case value means less recovery for a client who may need it most.

## Evidence

- In Case A, the client had only one treatment provider and a significant gap. The adjuster cited the gap to justify the offer. After subtracting pre-settlement funding ($6,537) and reduced medical bills ($5,397), the client would net less than $3K from an $18K offer.
- In Case B, the client's doctor recommended chiropractic care, but the client could not coordinate treatment around work and parenting. The spouse acknowledged this was "an area of risk."

## Insight

Early intervention on treatment gaps could significantly improve case value. When a client indicates they may stop treatment, the firm should:
1. Document the practical barriers to treatment
2. Counsel the client on the impact of stopping treatment
3. Help find practical solutions (providers with flexible hours, telehealth options)
4. If treatment truly cannot continue, document the reasons thoroughly for the demand narrative

## Agent Guidance

1. Monitor treatment continuity — flag any gap exceeding 14 days
2. When a client indicates they want to stop treatment, escalate to the attorney
3. In the demand letter, proactively explain any treatment gap with supporting documentation
4. Track the treatment gap + financial obligations equation to predict net client recovery early

## Related

- [[treatment-gap-defense]] — the adjuster tactic this connection feeds
- [[client-expectations-management]] — clients need to understand the impact
- [[pre-settlement-funding-obligations]] — funding compounds the low-value problem
"}

# PIP Processing Delays Cause Collection Anxiety

> This connection documents how delays in [[pip-claims-management]] directly cause [[medical-bill-collections-anxiety]], which in turn creates pressure on [[client-expectations-management]] and can push clients toward premature settlement.

## Connection

Three concepts form a causal chain:

1. **PIP Processing Delays** ([[pip-claims-management]]): Bills submitted to PIP may sit unprocessed for 30-60+ days. In one observed case, NO payments were made after 60 days.

2. **Bills Go to Collections** ([[medical-bill-collections-anxiety]]): Medical providers typically send bills to collections after 60-90 days of non-payment. If PIP hasn't paid, the bill goes to collections while the firm is still managing the case.

3. **Client Pressure** ([[client-expectations-management]]): Clients receive collection notices and become anxious about their credit. They contact the firm with increasing urgency, eventually expressing desire to "put this to bed" regardless of case value.

This chain creates a situation where administrative delays in PIP processing directly undermine the firm's negotiating position by creating client urgency to settle.

## Evidence

- In Case C, bills were submitted to State Farm PIP on 11/1/2024 but no payments were made as of 12/31/2024. Meanwhile, the client received collection notices from Credit Bureau Systems and came into the office concerned about his credit.
- In Case B, the client's husband forwarded collection notices in November 2025 and expressed urgency: "I don't want this to hurt our credit" and "We want this to go away." This coincided with outstanding PIP processing.

## Insight

Aggressive PIP follow-up is one of the highest-leverage activities a paralegal can perform. Reducing PIP processing time from 60+ days to 30 days would eliminate most collection anxiety, which in turn would reduce client pressure for premature settlement.

## Prevention Protocol

1. Submit bills to PIP within 48 hours of receipt
2. Set a 14-day follow-up task (not 30 days) for each PIP submission
3. If no confirmation of processing at 14 days, call the PIP adjuster
4. Proactively inform clients: "We have submitted your bill to your auto insurance for payment. This typically takes 2-4 weeks."
5. If a collection notice arrives, respond within 24 hours with status and timeline

## Related

- [[pip-claims-management]] — the root cause of the delay
- [[medical-bill-collections-anxiety]] — the symptom experienced by clients
- [[client-expectations-management]] — proactive communication prevents escalation
"}

# Records Completion Gates Demand Preparation

> This connection documents how delays in [[medical-records-collection]] create the primary bottleneck preventing [[case-phase-transitions]] from Treatment/Demand in Progress to Negotiation. The demand cannot be sent until records are complete, and records delays cascade into client frustration.

## Connection

The dependency chain:

1. **Treatment Completion**: Client finishes treatment (or decides to stop)
2. **Records Requests**: Firm sends records/billing requests to all providers
3. **Wait Period**: Providers take 1-6+ weeks to respond
4. **Completeness Check**: All providers must have returned both records AND bills
5. **Demand Preparation**: Only after completeness can the demand be drafted
6. **Attorney Review**: Attorney reviews and approves demand
7. **Demand Sent**: Case moves to Negotiation

Any delay at step 3 delays everything downstream. And the client experiences the entire wait as "nothing is happening."

## Evidence

- In Case B, treatment was confirmed complete in late October 2025. Records from St. Elizabeth came back same day, but records from Christ Hospital Physicians took significantly longer. The case was still in "Demand in Progress" as of March 2026 — nearly 5 months after treatment ended — partly due to records delays.
- In Case C, the attorney flagged that "we need a record and was sent to get this a month ago with no action" — records delays directly prevented negotiation progress.
- In Case B, the paralegal told the client's husband: "I'm still waiting for the complete records/billing from Christ Hospital Physicians Primary Care. Once I have both of those I can move this case towards resolution." This directly connects records delays to case resolution timeline.

## Insight

Records collection is the single biggest controllable bottleneck in case lifecycle. Reducing the average records turnaround from 4-6 weeks to 2 weeks would compress the overall case timeline by months.

## Agent Guidance

1. Send records requests the same day treatment is confirmed complete (or earlier if possible)
2. Use the fastest channel available for each provider (email for some, fax for others)
3. Set aggressive follow-up intervals: 7 days for the first follow-up, then every 7 days
4. Maintain a per-case tracker showing each provider's records status and bill status
5. When the attorney asks about case status, lead with the records tracker
6. When the client asks about timeline, explain which specific records are outstanding

## Related

- [[medical-records-collection]] — the process being optimized
- [[case-phase-transitions]] — the transitions that records completion unlocks
- [[attorney-review-triggers]] — stalled records should trigger attorney escalation
"}

{"path": "/opt/data/FirmVault/wiki/log.md", "content": "# Law Firm Wiki — Compilation Log

## 2026-04-12 — Initial Compilation

**Compiler**: Wiki Compiler Agent
**Cases Processed**: 3
**Source Data**:
- Case A (negotiation phase): 42 activity logs — transfer case from prior counsel, Indiana MVA, incarcerated client, pre-settlement funding, treatment gap, single provider
- Case B (treatment phase): 76 activity logs — new intake, treatment to demand transition, PIP management (Erie), BI claim (Progressive), multiple providers, engaged family contact
- Case C (negotiation phase): 225 activity logs — full lifecycle from intake through negotiation, PIP (State Farm), BI (State Farm), health insurance lien (Anthem), lien disputes, collections issues, SOL countdown

**Articles Created**: 12 concept articles, 3 connection articles
**Confidence Level**: All articles at "low" (1-3 cases each) — will improve as more cases are compiled

### Concepts Created
1. `intake-and-onboarding-workflow` — Standard intake workflow from initial contact through file setup
2. `file-transfer-from-prior-counsel` — Process for obtaining files from prior law firms
3. `treatment-gap-defense` — Adjuster tactic of citing treatment gaps to reduce value
4. `pip-claims-management` — PIP bill submission, tracking, and follow-up
5. `medical-records-collection` — Records/bills request process from providers
6. `client-communication-through-family` — Patterns when family members are primary contacts
7. `demand-sent-by-prior-firm` — Edge case when prior firm already sent a demand
8. `lien-management-health-insurance` — Health insurance subrogation lien audit and dispute
9. `pre-settlement-funding-obligations` — Lawsuit loans and their impact on net recovery
10. `case-phase-transitions` — Phase lifecycle and transition prerequisites
11. `attorney-review-triggers` — Events requiring attorney involvement
12. `client-expectations-management` — Managing client anxiety and timeline expectations
13. `medical-bill-collections-anxiety` — Bills going to collections during case
14. `statute-of-limitations-monitoring` — SOL countdown and escalation

### Connections Created
1. `treatment-gaps-affect-settlement-value` — How treatment gaps + funding obligations create low-value outcomes
2. `pip-delays-cause-collections-anxiety` — How PIP delays cascade into collections and client pressure
3. `records-completion-gates-demand` — How records delays bottleneck the entire case timeline

### Key Patterns Identified
- **PIP is the biggest operational bottleneck**: Bills submitted to PIP can sit unprocessed for 60+ days, causing downstream collection issues
- **Treatment gap is the most common adjuster defense**: Observed in multiple cases, directly tied to lower offers
- **Client communication through family is the norm, not the exception**: 2 of 3 cases had a family member as primary contact
- **Records collection delays cascade**: Every week of delay in records extends the overall case timeline
- **Attorney involvement is episodic, not continuous**: The attorney checks in periodically and makes directional decisions, but daily work is paralegal-driven
- **Phase transitions are often delayed**: Cases can sit in a phase for weeks/months past when they should have transitioned

### Gaps Identified
- No settlement disbursement examples
- No demand letter construction examples
- No negotiation counter-offer exchanges
- No litigation workflow
- Limited data on provider billing negotiations
- No UIM (Underinsured Motorist) claim examples despite one case having a UIM claim
"}
